title: Parameter Sweeping and Visualisation
text: can bypass automated base parameter calculation nlmixr2autoinit instead specify CLCL, VdV_d, KaK_a directly. useful interested exploring complex model parameters VmaxV_{max} KmK_m, without re-running full estimation pipeline. example, sim_sens_1cmpt_mm() performs sweep VmaxV_{max} KmK_m one-compartment model Michaelis-Menten elimination IV data. can inspect first rows simulated output see multiple goodness--fit metrics returned parameter combination. Definitions metrics provided ?metrics.. focus rRMSE2, symmetric relative root mean squared error, normalizes predicted–observed difference mean predicted-observed pair used compare parameter sets.
code: library(nlmixr2autoinit) #> Loading required package: nlmixr2data # Example: IV dosing scenario with automatic Vmax and Km out1 <- sim_sens_1cmpt_mm(   dat    = Bolus_1CPTMM,   sim_vmax = list(mode = "manual", values = c(200, 400, 600, 800, 1000, 1200)),   sim_km   = list(mode = "manual", values = c(5, 10, 50, 100, 200, 400)),   sim_vd   = list(mode = "manual", values = 70),   sim_ka   = list(mode = "manual", values = NA),   route = "iv" ) head(out1) #> # A tibble: 6 × 11 #>    Vmax    Km    Vd    Ka      APE   MAE  MAPE  RMSE rRMSE1 rRMSE2 #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl> #> 1   200     5    70    NA 5572697.  802.  608. 1400.   87.9   73.9 #> 2   200    10    70    NA 5594331.  805.  621. 1402.   88.0   73.6 #> 3   200    50    70    NA 5753722.  828.  729. 1413.   88.8   76.8 #> 4   200   100    70    NA 5899008.  849.  821. 1425.   89.5   79.3 #> 5   200   200    70    NA 6096946.  877.  933. 1443.   90.6   81.9 #> 6   200   400    70    NA 6344339   913. 1059. 1468.   92.2   84.4 #> # ℹ 1 more variable: Cumulative.Time.Sec <dbl> # # A tibble: 6 × 11 #    Vmax    Km    Vd    Ka      APE   MAE  MAPE  RMSE rRMSE1 rRMSE2 Cumulative.Time.Sec #   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>               <dbl> # 1   200     5    70    NA 5572697.  802.  608. 1400.   87.9   73.9                0.33 # 2   200    10    70    NA 5594331.  805.  621. 1402.   88.0   73.6                0.64 # 3   200    50    70    NA 5753722.  828.  729. 1413.   88.8   76.8                0.99 # 4   200   100    70    NA 5899008.  849.  821. 1425.   89.5   79.3                1.29 # 5   200   200    70    NA 6096946.  877.  933. 1443.   90.6   81.9                1.6  # 6   200   400    70    NA 6344339   913. 1059. 1468.   92.2   84.4                1.92

title: Parameter Sweeping and Visualisation
text: can visualize sweep results heatmap examine model fit changes across combinations VmaxV_{max} KmK_m, lower rRMSE2 indicating better fit. example, optimal parameter combination identified Vmax=1000mg/hV_{max}=1000 mg/h Km=200mg/LK_m=200 mg/L, gives lowest rRMSE2 among tested values.
code: library(ggplot2) best <- out1[which.min(out1$rRMSE2), ] p1<-ggplot(out1, aes(x = factor(Vmax), y = factor(Km), fill = rRMSE2)) +   geom_tile() +   geom_tile(     data = best,     aes(x = factor(Vmax), y = factor(Km)),     fill  = NA,     color = "black",     linewidth = 1.2   ) +   geom_text(aes(label = round(rRMSE2, 1)), size = 4.5, color = "grey20") +   scale_fill_gradient(     low  = "#1a9641",     high = "#f7f7f7",     name = "rRMSE2"   ) +   labs(     x = expression(V[max]),     y = expression(K[m])   ) +   theme_bw() +   theme(     axis.title = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"),     axis.text  = element_text(size = 10)   ) print(p1)

title: Running nlmixr2autoinit
text: nlmixr2autoinit tool automatically generating initial parameter estimates PopPK models, using adaptive single-point methods, naïve pooled NCA, graphic methods, simulation-based parameter sweeping initialize structural statistical parameters. Details methods described https://doi.org/10.1007/s10928-025-10000-z.

title: Running nlmixr2autoinit
text: get started, need: concentration–time dataset standard nlmixr2-style format. key required columns : Additional columns MDV (missing dependent variable) ADDL/II (additional doses/interdose interval) supported present. can inspect built-example datasets see expected structure:

title: Running nlmixr2autoinit
text: nlmixr2autoinit compatible wide range PK data types study designs. Dose routes Dosing regimens Data types Mixed dose routes datasets subjects share single identical time point supported.

title: Running nlmixr2autoinit
text: Call getPPKinits() dataset. function automatically detects route administration data characteristics. review detected data information: recommended initial estimates, automatically selected algorithm, can accessed directly via:
code: library(nlmixr2autoinit) outs <- getPPKinits(dat = theo_sd) outs$Datainfo #>                               Infometrics      Value #> 1                              Dose Route       oral #> 2                               Dose Type first_dose #> 3                      Number of Subjects         12 #> 4                  Number of Observations        123 #> 5  Subjects with First-Dose Interval Data         12 #> 6 Observations in the First-Dose Interval        123 #> 7        Subjects with Multiple-Dose Data          0 #> 8       Observations after Multiple Doses          0 outs$Recommended_initial_estimates #            Parameters               Methods   Values # 1                  Ka Naive pooled NCA (FD)   1.5800 # 2                  CL Naive pooled NCA (FD)   3.0400 # 3                  Vd Naive pooled NCA (FD)  33.0000 # 4                Vmax    Parameter sweeping 105.3596 # 5                  Km    Parameter sweeping  35.0367 # 6           Vc(2CMPT)    Parameter sweeping  31.9000 # 7           Vp(2CMPT)    Parameter sweeping   3.1900 # 8            Q(2CMPT)    Parameter sweeping   6.0800 # 9           Vc(3CMPT)    Parameter sweeping  31.9000 # 10          Vp(3CMPT)    Parameter sweeping   3.1900 # 11         Vp2(3CMPT)    Parameter sweeping   3.1900 # 12           Q(3CMPT)    Parameter sweeping   6.0800 # 13          Q2(3CMPT)    Parameter sweeping   6.0800 # 14     Sigma additive           Model-based   0.1065 # 15 Sigma proportional           Model-based   0.0262

title: Running nlmixr2autoinit
text: $Run.history records every method combination attempted evaluated pipeline. access component, use <outname>$Run.history$<component>. example, estimation details base PK parameters (CLCL, VdV_d, KaK_a) can accessed via: can see row represents unique combination estimation methods KaK_a, CLCL, VdV_d, combination performs resulting parameters used predict observed concentration–time data allowing best-performing combination selected automatically. Note: metrics.rank ranks method combinations predictive performance (rank 1 = best), based metric specified initsControl() (default: rRMSE2). combination rank 1 used recommended initial estimates. min_count indicates many metrics simultaneously minimum given method combination multiple metrics used selection criteria.

title: Running nlmixr2autoinit
text: getPPKinits() accepts control argument via initsControl() customise pipeline behaviour. example, can enable fallback value Ka=1K_a = 1, nlmixr2autoinit assign Ka=1K_a = 1 default algorithm unable compute automatically dataset: See ?initsControl full list options.
code: inits.out <- getPPKinits(   dat = theo_md,   control = initsControl(     fallback.control = fallback_control(enable_ka_fallback = TRUE)   ) )

title: Running nlmixr2autoinit
text: Integration nlmixr2 — learn pass initial estimates directly nlmixr2 model definition. Integration nlmixr2auto — trigger automated model selection nlmixr2auto. Parameter Sweeping Visualisation — explore parameter sweeping works visualise sweep results.

title: Integration with nlmixr2
text: nlmixr2autoinit designed slot naturally nlmixr2 ecosystem. initial estimates generated getPPKinits(), can extracted used write model function initial values pre-filled automatically, ready pass nlmixr2 fitting.

title: Integration with nlmixr2
text: starting point getPPKinits(), analyses dataset returns recommended initial estimates: pheno_sd, primary structural parameters CL = 0.0087, Vd = 1.25 Sigma additive = 5.0434.
code: library(nlmixr2autoinit) inits.out <- getPPKinits(pheno_sd) inits.out$Recommended_initial_estimates #            Parameters                      Methods   Values # 1                  Ka                           IV       NA # 2                  CL Adaptive single-point method   0.0087 # 3                  Vd Adaptive single-point method   1.2500 # 4                Vmax           Parameter sweeping   1.0572 # 5                  Km           Parameter sweeping 120.0145 # 6           Vc(2CMPT)           Parameter sweeping   1.2500 # 7           Vp(2CMPT)           Parameter sweeping   0.1250 # 8            Q(2CMPT)           Parameter sweeping   0.0174 # 9           Vc(3CMPT)           Parameter sweeping   1.2500 # 10          Vp(3CMPT)           Parameter sweeping   0.1250 # 11         Vp2(3CMPT)           Parameter sweeping   0.1250 # 12           Q(3CMPT)           Parameter sweeping   0.0174 # 13          Q2(3CMPT)           Parameter sweeping   0.0174 # 14     Sigma additive    Fallback (fixed fraction)   5.0434 # 15 Sigma proportional    Fallback (fixed fraction)   0.2000

title: Integration with nlmixr2
text: values Recommended_initial_estimates can read directly ini block. pheno_sd, one-compartment IV bolus model : can also extract estimates named variables first, reference directly ini block: Print summary model fit results: plan run multiple model structures, can extract parameters upfront reuse across models without repeating lookup: Print summary model fit results:
code: ini({   tcl    <- log(0.0087)  # typical value of clearance (log scale)   tv     <- log(1.25)    # typical value of volume (log scale)    eta.cl + eta.v ~ c(1,                      0.01, 1)    add.err <- 5.0434      # additive residual variability }) est <- inits.out$Recommended_initial_estimates CL      = as.numeric(est$Values[est$Parameters == "CL"]) Vd      = as.numeric(est$Values[est$Parameters == "Vd"]) add_err = as.numeric(est$Values[est$Parameters == "Sigma additive"])  pheno <- function() {   ini({     tcl     <-   log(CL)      tv      <-   log(Vd)       eta.cl + eta.v ~ c(0.1,                         0.01, 0.1)     add.err <- add_err   })   model({     cl <- exp(tcl + eta.cl)     v  <- exp(tv  + eta.v)     ke <- cl / v     d/dt(A1) = -ke * A1     cp = A1 / v     cp ~ add(add.err)   }) } fit <- nlmixr2(pheno, pheno_sd, est = "saem",                 control = saemControl(print = 0)) print(fit) # ── nlmixr² SAEM OBJF by FOCEi approximation ── #  #  Gaussian/Laplacian Likelihoods: AIC(fit) or fit$objf etc.  #  FOCEi CWRES & Likelihoods: addCwres(fit)  #  # ── Time (sec fit$time): ── #  #            setup covariance  saem table compress    other # elapsed 0.002899   0.025013 8.602 0.117    0.046 1.968088 #  # ── Population Parameters (fit$parFixed or fit$parFixedDf): ── #  #          Est.     SE %RSE   Back-transformed(95%CI) BSV(CV%) Shrink(SD)% # tcl     -5.02 0.0763 1.52 0.00662 (0.0057, 0.00768)     52.7      4.18%  # tv      0.349 0.0548 15.7         1.42 (1.27, 1.58)     41.9      1.55%  # add.err  2.74                                  2.74                      #   #   Covariance Type (fit$covMethod): linFim #   Correlations in between subject variability (BSV) matrix: #     cor:eta.v,eta.cl  #           0.927   #   #  #   Full BSV covariance (fit$omega) or correlation (fit$omegaR; diagonals=SDs)  #   Distribution stats (mean/skewness/kurtosis/p-value) available in fit$shrink  #   Censoring (fit$censInformation): No censoring # title: nlmixr2autoinit - Automatic Initial Estimates for Population PK Modelling
text: nlmixr2autoinit provides automated framework deriving initial parameter estimates population pharmacokinetic (PopPK) models via multiple PK analysis methods. aims streamline model parameter initialization R, reducing reliance manual trial--error facilitating automated model development workflows.

title: Fit intravenous pharmacokinetic data to a one-compartment linear elimination model — Fit_1cmpt_iv
text: Fits intravenous (IV) pharmacokinetic data one-compartment model first-order elimination using naive pooled data approach. Supports multiple estimation methods provided nlmixr2 can optionally return predicted concentrations support efficient simulation workflows.

title: Fit intravenous pharmacokinetic data to a one-compartment linear elimination model — Fit_1cmpt_iv
text: data data frame IV pharmacokinetic data formatted nlmixr2. est.method Estimation method use nlmixr2. Must one : "rxSolve", "nls", "nlm", "nlminb", "focei". input.cl Initial estimate clearance (CL). input.vd Initial estimate volume distribution (V). input.add Initial estimate additive residual error. return.pred.Logical; TRUE, returns data frame predicted concentrations (cp) observations input data. ... Additional arguments passed nlmixr2(), user-defined control = foceiControl(...) control settings.

title: Fit intravenous pharmacokinetic data to a one-compartment linear elimination model — Fit_1cmpt_iv
text: return.pred.= TRUE, returns data.frame single column cp (predicted concentrations). Otherwise, returns fitted model object produced nlmixr2.

text: dat data frame containing pharmacokinetic data, including observed concentrations (DV), time dose (tad), dose, route information. half_life elimination half-life (t1/2) compound, used identify early-phase Cmax values. single_point_base.lst Optional list object returned run_single_point_base(). supplied, function generate internally. route Route administration. One "bolus", "oral", "infusion" (default = "bolus"). dose_type Optional string specifying dosing type, passed run_single_point_base(). pooled_ctrl Control object created pooled_control(), defining data pooling options. ssctrl Control object created ss_control(), defining steady-state control options.

title: Approximate volume of distribution from observed Cmax — approx.vc
text: list containing individual population Vd estimates related dose-level data.

title: Approximate volume of distribution from observed Cmax — approx.vc
text: Estimates individual apparent volumes distribution observed peak concentrations. Individual estimates summarized obtain population-level value. single-dose data, Vd calculated according route administration: Bolus: \(V_d = \mathrm{Dose} / C_{\mathrm{max}}\) Infusion: \(V_d = (\mathrm{Rate} \times t_{\mathrm{inf}}) / C_{\mathrm{max}}\) Oral: \(V_d = (\mathrm{Dose} \times F) / C_{\mathrm{max}}\), \(F = 1 - e^{-k_a t}\) multiple-dose data, observed Cmax values adjusted single-dose equivalents using accumulation ratio: $$R_{\mathrm{ac}} = \frac{1}{1 - e^{-k_e \tau}}, \quad k_e = \ln(2)/t_{1/2}$$ Adjusted values used estimate Vd using route-specific equations.

title: Calculate clearance using an adaptive single-point method — calculate_cl
text: dat data frame containing pharmacokinetic data. Required columns typically include ID, TIME, DV, tad, recent_ii, dose, routeobs, durationobs. half_life Optional numeric value drug's half-life. provided, half-life estimated using get_hf() pooled observations. dose_type Specifies dosing context pharmacokinetic observations. Required half_life provided. title: Calculate clearance using an adaptive single-point method — calculate_cl
text: list containing: dat: processed dataset steady-state identification cl_df: individual clearance estimates trimmed_mean_cl: population clearance calculated trimmed geometric mean 5 percent trimming level reduce impact outliers

title: Calculate clearance using an adaptive single-point method — calculate_cl
text: Estimates individual population clearance steady-state pharmacokinetic data. half-life provided, estimated pooled data using get_hf() pooling rules defined pooled_control(). procedure: Identifies steady-state observations using is_ss() ss_control() criteria. Selects peak trough concentrations within dose interval represent steady-state behavior. Classifies concentration points Cssmax, Cssmin, Cssavg based timing within interval decay pattern. Computes individual clearance Dose / (Cssavg × tau). Aggregates individual clearance values using trimmed geometric mean obtain population estimate. Supports bolus, infusion, oral administration routes.

what: Find the best terminal elimination rate constant (lambdaz) — find_best_lambdaz
title: Find the best terminal elimination rate constant (lambdaz) — find_best_lambdaz
text: Identifies optimal terminal phase lambdaz estimation using systematic log-linear regression approach adjusted R-squared optimization criteria.

title: Find the best terminal elimination rate constant (lambdaz) — find_best_lambdaz
code: find_best_lambdaz(   time,   conc,   route = "bolus",   duration = NULL,   adj_r_squared_threshold = 0.7,   nlastpoints = 3,   tolerance = 1e-04 )

title: Find the best terminal elimination rate constant (lambdaz) — find_best_lambdaz
text: time Numeric vector observation time points. conc Numeric vector concentration measurements corresponding time points. route Administration method specification: "bolus" (default) - Excludes time maximum concentration (Tmax) point "infusion" - Includes Tmax point terminal phase evaluation "oral" - Starts regression Tmax point duration Numeric (optional). Duration infusion administration, time units time. Required route = "infusion". Used determine first post-infusion observation time terminal phase regression. adj_r_squared_threshold Minimum acceptable adjusted R-squared value valid estimation (default = 0.7). Values threshold generate warnings. nlastpoints Integer. Minimum number terminal points (end profile) include evaluating candidate regression segments \(\lambda_z\). Default 3. tolerance Threshold considering adjusted R-squared values statistically equivalent (default = 1e-4). Used selecting fits similar goodness--fit.

title: Find the best terminal elimination rate constant (lambdaz) — find_best_lambdaz
text: list containing: lambdaz: Estimated terminal elimination rate constant (\(\lambda_z\)), NA valid fit UsedPoints: Number data points used optimal fit adj.r.squared: Adjusted R-squared value (\(R^2\)) optimal regression message: Character vector containing diagnostic messages warnings

title: Find the best terminal elimination rate constant (lambdaz) — find_best_lambdaz
text: algorithm implements following decision logic: Identifies time maximum observed concentration (Tmax) Defines candidate terminal phases starting last 3 measurable concentrations Iteratively evaluates longer time spans including preceding data points candidate phase: Performs log-concentration vs. time linear regression Requires negative regression slope (positive \(\lambda_z\)) Calculates adjusted R-squared metric Selects optimal phase based : Highest adjusted R-squared value R-squared differences < tolerance, selects fit points Validates final selection R-squared threshold

title: Find the best terminal elimination rate constant (lambdaz) — find_best_lambdaz
text: Zhonghui Huang

title: Find the best terminal elimination rate constant (lambdaz) — find_best_lambdaz

what: Forceful estimation of terminal slope — force_find_lambdaz
title: Forceful estimation of terminal slope — force_find_lambdaz
text: Estimates terminal elimination rate constant (lambda_z) pharmacokinetic profile. function first attempts use find_best_lambdaz method. valid estimate obtained, falls back simplified log-linear regression using progressively fewer data points enforce negative slope.

title: Forceful estimation of terminal slope — force_find_lambdaz
code: force_find_lambdaz(time, conc, ...)

title: Forceful estimation of terminal slope — force_find_lambdaz
text: time Numeric vector time points. conc Numeric vector concentration values corresponding time. ... Additional arguments passed find_best_lambdaz (e.g., nlastpoints).

title: Forceful estimation of terminal slope — force_find_lambdaz
text: list containing: lambdaz: Estimated terminal elimination rate constant (1/time) intercept: Intercept log-linear regression, used extrapolate concentration time zero method: Method used (find_best_lambdaz fallback regression) UsedPoints: Number time-concentration points used estimation adj.r.squared: Adjusted R-squared (available using find_best_lambdaz) message: Diagnostic message summarizing outcome slopefit: Fitted linear model object

title: Forceful estimation of terminal slope — force_find_lambdaz
text: function implements two-step strategy ensure estimation terminal elimination slope: First, applies find_best_lambdaz automatically select best fitting terminal phase segment based adjusted R-squared optimization. find_best_lambdaz fails (e.g., limited data), function forcibly fits simplified linear models using progressively fewer points (starting n-1 2) negative slope identified. fallback mode, adjusted R-squared considered.

what: Automated pipeline for generating initial estimates in population PK models — getPPKinits
title: Automated pipeline for generating initial estimates in population PK models — getPPKinits
text: Provides unified fully automated workflow generate initial pharmacokinetic residual variability parameters population PK models using concentration–time data bolus, infusion, oral administration.

title: Automated pipeline for generating initial estimates in population PK models — getPPKinits
code: getPPKinits(dat, control = initsControl(), verbose = TRUE)

title: Automated pipeline for generating initial estimates in population PK models — getPPKinits
text: dat data frame containing pharmacokinetic records standard nlmixr2 format, including ID, TIME, EVID, DV. control list created initsControl() specifying configuration pooling, non-compartmental analysis, steady-state detection, fallback rules, statistical model components, parameter selection metrics. verbose Logical (default = TRUE); TRUE, displays key progress messages stepwise updates initialization process. FALSE, function runs quietly without printing intermediate information.

title: Automated pipeline for generating initial estimates in population PK models — getPPKinits
text: object class getPPKinits containing recommended initial parameter estimates, intermediate results, computation diagnostics.

title: Automated pipeline for generating initial estimates in population PK models — getPPKinits
text: pipeline integrates four model-informed analytical components applied raw pooled concentration–time profiles: Adaptive single-point methods Naive pooled graphic methods Naive pooled non-compartmental analysis (NCA) optional Wagner–Nelson Ka calculation oral dosing Parameter sweeping across one-, two-, three-compartment Michaelis–Menten models addition structural PK parameters, framework also initializes statistical model components: Inter-individual variability (IIV): pragmatic fixed \(\omega^2\) values assigned random effects. Residual unexplained variability (RUV): estimated either using data-driven method based trimmed residual summaries fixed-fraction approach consistent NONMEM User Guide recommendations. Model applicability: automated model-informed strategy generates robust initial values suitable linear nonlinear mixed-effects pharmacokinetic models.

code: getnca(   x,   y,   dose = 1,   trapezoidal.rule = c("linear_up_log_down", "linear"),   ss = 0,   duration = NULL,   nlastpoints = 3,   slope.method = c("bestfitforce", "bestfit"),   route = c("bolus", "oral", "infusion") )

title: Perform non-compartmental pharmacokinetic analysis — getnca
text: x Numeric vector observation times. y Numeric vector drug concentration measurements. dose Administered dose (default = 1). trapezoidal.rule Method AUC calculation: "linear" - Linear trapezoidal method (default) "linear_up_log_down" - Linear-/log-method (linear ascending concentrations, logarithmic descending) ss Steady-state flag: 0 - Use extrapolated \(AUC_{0 \rightarrow \infty}\) clearance (default) 1 - Use observed \(AUC_{0 \rightarrow \mathrm{last}}\) clearance duration Infusion duration (required route = "infusion"). nlastpoints Number terminal points slope estimation (default = 3). slope.method Method estimating terminal slope (\(\lambda_z\)): "bestfitforce" - Force estimation using decreasing number terminal points best-fit fails (default) "bestfit" - Use automated best-fit selection based adjusted R-squared route Administration route: "bolus" - Intravenous bolus (default) "oral" - Oral administration "infusion" - Intravenous infusion

title: Perform non-compartmental pharmacokinetic analysis — getnca
text: list containing: cl - Clearance (CL), calculated Dose/AUC vz - volume distribution (Vz), calculated CL / lambdaz half_life - Terminal elimination half-life, computed ln(2) / lambdaz auct - Area concentration–time curve time 0 last measurable concentration auc0_inf - AUC extrapolated infinity C_last - Last non-zero measurable concentration lambdaz - Terminal elimination rate constant aumc_0_t - Area first moment curve time 0 last measurable concentration aumc_0_inf - AUMC extrapolated infinity used_points - Number time–concentration points used estimate lambdaz adj.r.squared - Adjusted R-squared terminal phase regression messages - Warning diagnostic messages returned calculation

what: Estimate individual-level residual error from the elimination phase — getsigmas
title: Estimate individual-level residual error from the elimination phase — getsigmas

title: Estimate individual-level residual error from the elimination phase — getsigmas
code: getsigmas(group_df, nlastpoints = 3)

title: Estimate individual-level residual error from the elimination phase — getsigmas
text: group_df data frame single group (e.g., one subject dose), containing columns: EVID (event ID), DV (observed concentration), TIME (time dose), routeobs (administration route). nlastpoints Integer specifying number terminal data points used regression.

title: Estimate individual-level residual error from the elimination phase — getsigmas
text: tibble following columns: intercept: Intercept log-linear regression line slope: Estimate terminal elimination rate constant residual_sd_additive: Standard deviation additive residuals residual_sd_proportional: Standard deviation proportional residuals

title: Estimate individual-level residual error from the elimination phase — getsigmas
text: Residuals computed individual-predicted concentrations (IPRED) observed concentrations (DV) using following definitions: $$   \sigma_{add} = \sqrt{Var(C_{obs} - C_{pred})} $$ $$   \sigma_{prop} = \sqrt{Var\left(\frac{C_{obs}}{C_{pred}} - 1\right)} $$ \(C_{obs}\) observed concentration \(C_{pred}\) model-predicted concentration obtained back-transformation log-linear regression. additive residual standard deviation (\(\sigma_{add}\)) proportional residual standard deviation (\(\sigma_{prop}\)) calculated per individual.

title: Graphical calculation of pharmacokinetic parameters for oral administration — graphcal_oral
text: terminal slope (lambdaz) estimated using force_find_lambdaz(). apparent volume distribution clearance computed using following relationships: $$Vd/F = \frac{Dose \times ka}{C_0 \times (ka - kel)}$$ $$Cl/F = kel \times Vd/F$$ ka estimated absorption phase.

Fbio Numeric. Bioavailability fraction (default = 1, meaning 100% bioavailability). Dose Numeric. Administered dose drug (mg). tau Numeric. Dosing interval (hours) successive doses.

title: Calculate absorption rate constant (ka) in a multiple-dose one-compartment model — ka_calculation_md
text: list containing following components: ka calculated absorption rate constant. full_solution full solution object returned uniroot() function, includes additional details root-finding process.

title: Calculate absorption rate constant (ka) in a multiple-dose one-compartment model — ka_calculation_md
text: value ka obtained numerically using uniroot unction solving following equation: $$Ct = \frac{Fbio \cdot Dose \cdot ka}{Vd \cdot (ka - ke)} \left( \frac{e^{-ke \cdot t}}{1 - e^{-ke \cdot \tau}} - \frac{e^{-ka \cdot t}}{1 - e^{-ka \cdot \tau}} \right)$$ ka estimated using uniroot(), solves root residual function (predicted Ct - observed Ct) within bounded interval (ka > ke ka <= 1000)

title: Calculate the absorption rate constant using the Wagner-Nelson method — ka_wanger_nelson
text: Wagner-Nelson method estimates fraction drug absorbed time based principle mass balance, unabsorbed fraction quantified proportion administered dose yet entered systemic circulation. linear regression applied natural logarithm unabsorbed fraction versus time, negative slope regression corresponds first-order absorption rate constant 'ka'. Key assumptions: Single-dose oral extravascular administration First-order absorption first-order elimination Linear pharmacokinetics 'ka' greater 'ke' Computational steps: AUC calculated using trapezoidal integration. fraction absorbed calculated AUC terminal elimination rate constant. remaining fraction transformed using natural logarithm. Linear regression log(remaining fraction) time yields 'ka'.

text: Computes common error metrics quantify predictive performance pharmacometric models comparing predicted (pred.x) observed (obs.y) concentration values.

title: Calculate metrics for model predictive performance evaluation — metrics.

title: Calculate metrics for model predictive performance evaluation — metrics.
text: pred.x Numeric vector model-predicted values. obs.y Numeric vector corresponding observed values.

title: Calculate metrics for model predictive performance evaluation — metrics.
text: numeric vector named elements: APE: absolute prediction error MAE: mean absolute error MAPE: mean absolute percentage error RMSE: root mean squared error rRMSE1: relative RMSE (type 1) rRMSE2: relative RMSE (type 2)

title: Calculate metrics for model predictive performance evaluation — metrics.
text: function stops error pred.x obs.y unequal lengths. following metrics calculated: $$APE = \sum |pred.x - obs.y|$$ Absolute prediction error (APE) sum absolute differences. $$MAE = \frac{1}{n} \sum |pred.x - obs.y|$$ Mean absolute error (MAE) expresses average absolute deviation. $$MAPE = \frac{100}{n} \sum \left| \frac{pred.x - obs.y}{obs.y} \right|$$ Mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) normalizes error observed values. $$RMSE = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum (pred.x - obs.y)^2}$$ Root mean squared error (RMSE) penalizes larger deviations. $$rRMSE1 = \frac{RMSE}{\bar{obs.y}} \times 100$$ Relative RMSE type 1 RMSE normalized mean observed value. $$rRMSE2 = 100 \times \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum \left( \frac{pred.x - obs.y}{(pred.x + obs.y)/2} \right)^2}$$ Relative RMSE type 2 symmetric normalizes mean predicted–observed pair.

title: Calculate metrics for model predictive performance evaluation — metrics.
code: obs.y  <- rnorm(100, mean = 100, sd = 10) pred.x <- obs.y + rnorm(100, mean = 0, sd = 5) metrics.(pred.x = pred.x, obs.y = obs.y) #>    metrics.ape    metrics.mae   metrics.mape   metrics.rmse metrics.rrmse1  #>     391.314477       3.913145       3.962923       4.779439       4.791252  #> metrics.rrmse2  #>       4.853167

title: Process time–concentration dataset for pharmacokinetic analysis — processData
text: function standardizes preprocesses time–concentration data ensure compatibility pharmacokinetic modeling workflows nlmixr2. operations follow steps: Standardize data convert column names uppercase coerce key columns (TIME, DV, EVID, AMT, RATE, etc.) numeric Process events observations impute EVID MDV missing handle censored data (CENS) converting excluded records remove recode invalid EVID values (e.g., DV = 0 observations) Expand dose events expand dosing records using nmpkconvert() ADDL II present assign dose occasions using mark_dose_number() Determine administration route infusion logic derive RATE DUR needed identify route (bolus, infusion, oral) based compartment rate Generate derived variables calculate time dose using calculate_tad() compute dose-normalized concentration (DVstd) flag eligible records terminal elimination phase Summarize dataset classify dataset first-dose, repeated-dose, mixed generate summary metrics nlmixr2 analysis Classification dosing context based pharmacokinetic observation records (EVID equal 0), determining whether observed concentrations occur first dose, repeated dosing, across contexts. categories : first_dose: observations occur initial administration, without repeated-dose steady-state intervals. repeated_doses: observations occur multiple administrations steady-state conditions. combined_doses: observations include first-dose repeated-dose intervals analyzed together.

title: Estimate the absorption rate constant using pointwise methods — run_ka_solution
text: df data frame containing raw time–concentration data standard nlmixr2 format. cl numeric value drug clearance. assumed constant across subjects unless pre-specified per subject. ke numeric value elimination rate constant. assumed known estimated terminal phase. Fbio numeric value bioavailability (F). Default 1.

title: Estimate the absorption rate constant using pointwise methods — run_ka_solution
text: list following elements: ka_calc_median Median ka value across valid observations. ka_calc_dat_sd Data frame containing absorption-phase single-dose data, estimated ka diagnostic messages. ka_calc_dat_md Data frame containing absorption-phase steady-state multiple-dose data, ka estimates. ka_calc_dat Combined data frame (single-dose multiple-dose) containing ka estimates.

title: Estimate the absorption rate constant using pointwise methods — run_ka_solution
text: subject, time maximum observed concentration (Tmax) identified time corresponding highest DV. records TIME less equal Tmax retained, representing absorption phase. Two scenario-specific calculations implemented: single-dose multiple-dose steady state. single-dose data (dose_number == 1 SteadyState == FALSE), function uses ka_calculation_sd(), applies one-compartment oral absorption model first-order absorption elimination. steady-state multiple-dose data (dose_number > 1 SteadyState == TRUE), function uses ka_calculation_md(), accounts accumulation using dosing interval (dose_interval). function perform model fitting. median recommended use pharmacokinetic modeling.

title: Run full adaptive single-point PK analysis — run_single_point
code: # Example: Adaptive single-point PK analysis for bolus data # Step 1: Preprocess the data dat <- Oral_1CPT out <- processData(dat) #>  #>  #> Infometrics                               Value           #> ----------------------------------------  --------------- #> Dose Route                                oral            #> Dose Type                                 combined_doses  #> Number of Subjects                        120             #> Number of Observations                    6947            #> Subjects with First-Dose Interval Data    120             #> Observations in the First-Dose Interval   2273            #> Subjects with Multiple-Dose Data          120             #> Observations after Multiple Doses         4674            #> ----------------------------------------  ------ # Step 2: Extract route and dose type info froute <- out$Datainfo$Value[out$Datainfo$Infometrics == "Dose Route"] fdose_type <- out$Datainfo$Value[out$Datainfo$Infometrics == "Dose Type"] # Step 3: Estimate half life half_life <- get_hf(dat = out$dat)$half_life_median #> Estimating half-life.................... #> Half-life estimation complete: Estimated t1/2 = 11.08 h # Step 4: Run single-point analysis (CL, Vd, Ka if oral) result <- run_single_point(   dat = out$dat,   route = froute,   dose_type = fdose_type,   half_life = half_life ) # Step 5: View results print(result$singlepoint.results) #>      ka   cl   vd           starttime time.spent #> 1 0.889 3.28 52.4 2026-05-08 00:59:36      1.527 #>                                                         single_point.message #> 1 CL and Vd were calculated directly from steady-state and single-dose data.

title: Perform extended single-point pharmacokinetic calculations — run_single_point_extra
text: function derives pharmacokinetic parameters using following logic: clearance (CL) volume distribution (Vd) available base calculation, values directly used without modification. Vd missing CL elimination half-life provided, Vd calculated using: $$V_d = \frac{CL \cdot t_{1/2}}{\ln(2)}$$ CL missing Vd half-life available, CL calculated using: $$CL = \frac{V_d \cdot \ln(2)}{t_{1/2}}$$ CL Vd unavailable half-life provided, Vd estimated using dose Cmax: $$V_d = \frac{\mathrm{Dose}}{C_{\mathrm{max}}}$$ CL subsequently derived: $$CL = \frac{V_d \cdot \ln(2)}{t_{1/2}}$$ oral administration, absorption rate constant (ka) estimated using solution-based approach implemented run_ka_solution(). concentration–time data absorption phase used, defined time dose (tad) less 20% terminal half-life occurring prior Tmax, absorption dominant process. function supports bolus, oral, infusion administration routes. oral dosing, data within absorption phase used estimate absorption rate constant (ka), specifically using concentration-time observations prior maximum concentration (Tmax).

what: Internal control builder for steady-state evaluation — ss_control
title: Internal control builder for steady-state evaluation — ss_control
text: Constructs list control parameters used is_ss() determine pharmacokinetic steady state.

title: Internal control builder for steady-state evaluation — ss_control
code: ss_control(   ss_method = c("combined", "fixed_doses", "half_life_based"),   no.doses = 5,   no.half_lives = 5,   allowed_interval_variation = 0.25,   allowed_dose_variation = 0.2,   min_doses_required = 3,   tad_rounding = TRUE )

title: Internal control builder for steady-state evaluation — ss_control
text: ss_method Character string specifying method used determine steady state. One : "combined" (default): uses smaller dose-based estimate (.doses) half-life-based estimate (.half_lives) "fixed_doses": considers steady state reached .doses administrations "half_life_based": uses drug half-life dosing interval estimate required number doses .doses Integer indicating number doses assumed necessary reach steady state using "fixed_doses" method part "combined" method. Default 5. .half_lives Integer indicating number half-lives required reach steady state using "half_life_based" "combined" method. Default 5. allowed_interval_variation Numeric value specifying acceptable fractional variation dose interval. example, 0.25 allows plus minus 25 percent variation. Default 0.25. allowed_dose_variation Numeric value specifying acceptable fractional variation dose amount. example, 0.20 allows plus minus 20 percent variation. Default 0.20. min_doses_required Integer specifying minimum number doses must administered regardless method. Default 3. tad_rounding Logical value. TRUE (default), rounding applied comparing time dose (tad) dosing intervals allow small numerical deviations.

title: Internal control builder for steady-state evaluation — ss_control
text: named list containing steady-state control parameters, typically passed ssctrl argument is_ss().

title: Internal control builder for steady-state evaluation — ss_control

title: Linear trapezoidal rule — trapezoidal_linear
text: Computes area curve (AUC) area moment curve (AUMC) using linear trapezoidal rule. moment = TRUE, function estimates AUMC integrating time * concentration.

title: Linear trapezoidal rule — trapezoidal_linear
code: trapezoidal_linear(x, y, moment = FALSE)

title: Linear trapezoidal rule — trapezoidal_linear
text: x numeric vector representing time points. y numeric vector representing corresponding concentration values time point. moment Logical. TRUE, computes AUMC integrating t * C(t) instead just C(t).

title: Linear trapezoidal rule — trapezoidal_linear
text: numeric value representing estimated AUC AUMC using linear trapezoidal rule.

title: Linear trapezoidal rule — trapezoidal_linear
code: x <- c(0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8) y <- c(12, 8, 5, 3, 2, 1) trapezoidal_linear(x, y)                # AUC #> [1] 27.5 trapezoidal_linear(x, y, moment = TRUE) # AUMC #> [1] 78.5

what: Linear-up and log-down trapezoidal rule — trapezoidal_linear_up_log_down
title: Linear-up and log-down trapezoidal rule — trapezoidal_linear_up_log_down
text: Computes area curve (AUC) area moment curve (AUMC) using hybrid trapezoidal rule. method uses linear interpolation increasing constant concentration segments, logarithmic interpolation decreasing segments.

title: Linear-up and log-down trapezoidal rule — trapezoidal_linear_up_log_down
code: trapezoidal_linear_up_log_down(x, y, moment = FALSE)

title: Linear-up and log-down trapezoidal rule — trapezoidal_linear_up_log_down
text: x numeric vector representing time points. y numeric vector representing corresponding concentration values time point. moment Logical. TRUE, computes AUMC integrating t * C(t) instead C(t).

title: Linear-up and log-down trapezoidal rule — trapezoidal_linear_up_log_down
text: numeric value representing estimated AUC AUMC using linear-/log-trapezoidal method.

title: Linear-up and log-down trapezoidal rule — trapezoidal_linear_up_log_down
text: moment = TRUE, function calculates area moment curve (AUMC), .e., integrates t * C(t) time instead just C(t).

title: Linear-up and log-down trapezoidal rule — trapezoidal_linear_up_log_down
code: x <- c(0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8) y <- c(0, 2, 8, 5, 3, 2, 1) trapezoidal_linear_up_log_down(x, y)                # AUC #> [1] 25.03139 trapezoidal_linear_up_log_down(x, y, moment = TRUE) # AUMC #> [1] 78.85055
